rulings.Case Title: LOURDES ZAMUDIO ET AL VS. CARDIOVASCULAR RESEARCH FOUNDATION, INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendant Cardiovascular Research Foundation'S Motion For Summary Judgment. (For Tentative Ruling Only)
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Thursday, August 30, 2018, Line 3, Defendant Cardiovascular Research Foundation'S Motion For Summary Judgment. (Part 2 of 2)(For Tentative Ruling Only) Because there is no causal nexus between Mr. Damhuis' tortious conduct and his work as a CRF employee, Mr. Damhuis' misconduct is not CRF's conduct. (Lisa M. v. Henry Mayo Newhall Memorial Hospital (1995) 12 Cal.4th 291.) The undisputed facts show that Mr. Damhuis and Ms. Zamudio had never met before the incident, Mr. Damhuis' role as CRF's education coordinator did not include interaction with hotel employees, and Mr. Hartman never asked Mr. Damhuis to enter Mr. Hartman's room. The fact that Mr. Damhuis was in the Hotel, and therefore able to commit the tort, because he was a CRF employee is insufficient to establish the required causal nexus to attribute his misconduct to CRF. Mr. Damhuis' intentional sexual assault is not fairly attributed to any aspect of Mr. Damhuis' employment with CRF. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for CRF is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. (end of part 2 of 2) =(302/HK)

rulings.Case Title: LOURDES ZAMUDIO ET AL VS. CARDIOVASCULAR RESEARCH FOUNDATION, INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendant Cardiovascular Research Foundation'S Motion For Summary Judgment
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Thursday, August 30, 2018, Line 3, Defendant Cardiovascular Research Foundation'S Motion For Summary Judgment. (part 1 of 2) Defendant Cardiovascular Research Foundation's motion for summary judgment on the sole cause of action for breach of contract in the second amended complaint filed by plaintiffs Lourdes Zamudio is granted. After review of the deposition testimony of Jessica van der Gaast, the court remains of the view that based on the undisputed facts CRF is entitled to judgment in its favor. The testimony of Ms. van der Gaast and other witnesses regarding their unexpressed understanding of the relevant language of the indemnification provision of the 2007 CRF-Marriott contract is inadmissible. (In re Tobacco Cases I (2010) 186 Cal. App. 4th 42, 47 ("A contract must be interpreted to give effect to the mutual, expressed intention of the parties. Where the parties have reduced their agreement to writing, their mutual intention is to be determined, whenever possible, from the language of the writing alone. [T]he parties' expressed objective intent, not their unexpressed subjective intent, governs." (citations and internal quotation marks omitted)). Assuming without deciding that the indemnity provision provides for a first party claim by a Marriott employee, the undisputed facts establish that the indemnity provision does not impose liability on CRF for Alexander Damhuis' intentional torts. Because there is no dispute about the admissible extrinsic evidence, the interpretation of the phrase "indemnifying party's ¿ willful misconduct" in the indemnity provision is an issue of law to be decided by this court, not an issue of fact to be decided by the trier of fact. (Jade Fashion & Co., Inc. v. Harkham Industries, Inc. (2014) 229 Cal. App. 45h 635, 651-52 ). While Ms. Zamudio's proffered interpretation that the indemnification provision covers all willful misconduct committed by a CRF employee at the Hotel is plausible, it is far less plausible than CRF's proffered interpretation that the indemnification provision only covers the willful misconduct committed by a CRF employee in the scope and course of his employment. By common parlance as well as longstanding California law, the conduct of an entity party such as CRF is limited to the conduct of its employees that was done in the course and scope of their employment. While CRF and the Hotel were free to expand the scope of CRF's liability, there is no indication in the language of the indemnification provision or in any of the admissible extrinsic evidence that they chose to do so. Nor is there any evidence that the parties mistakenly misused the language they agreed to or intended other language. (end of part 1 of 2) =(302/HK)

rulings.Case Title: JASMINA ZALIHIC VS. LYFT, INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion Allowing Leave To File Second Amended Complaint For Damages; Memorandum Of Points And Authorities In Support Thereof; Declaration Of Andrew W. Ratto In Support Thereof; [Proposed Order]
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Thursday, August 30, 2018, Line 6, PLAINTIFF JASMINA ZALIHIC's Motion Allowing Leave To File Second Amended Complaint For Damages. Plaintiff Jasmina Zalihic's motion for leave to file a second amended complaint is denied. The motion is not necessary to enable Ms. Zalihic to argue in opposition to defendants' summary motions and at trial that the common carrier standard of care applies to her negligence claims, which appears to be motivation for this motion. Ms. Zalihic seeks to add two new claims which she has entitled "Negligence Per Se - Common Carrier." Both new claims are based on the same facts as her existing claims entitled "Negligence." Negligence per se is not a separate cause of action from a negligence cause of action, but merely one way that a negligence cause of action can be proved. Even if negligence per se was a separate cause of action from negligence, Ms. Zalihic's proposed new claims are not proper negligence per se claims, but rather allegations that the standard of care that applies to her negligence claims is the elevated standard of care owed by a common carrier (CACI 902) rather than the basic standard of care (CACI 401). The denial of this motion does not preclude Ms. Zahilic from arguing in opposition to defendants' summary judgment motions or at trial that the elevated standard of care applies to her negligence claims. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Ms. Zahilic is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/HK)

rulings.Case Title: EMALATA TAKAPU VS. OPTIONE HEALTHCARE MANAGEMENT LLC A CALIFORNIA ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Plaintiff'S Notice Of Motion And Motion For Order To Amend Complaint
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Thursday, August 30, 2018, Line 7, PLAINTIFF EMALATA TAKAPU's Motion For Order To Amend Complaint Plaintiff Emalata Takapu's motion for leave to file a second amended complaint is denied. While California has a policy of liberally allowing amendments to pleadings, that policy does not apply where, as here, the opposing parties would suffer legal prejudice by the granting of leave to amend. Adding a new defendant to a case less than two weeks from trial is legally prejudicial to that defendant. Adding new damages allegations against an unrepresented entity defendant more than three months after its counsel withdrew and less than two weeks from trial is legally prejudicial to that defendant. Ms. Takapu fails to explain why she did not file this motion in anticipation of or immediately after the withdrawal of counsel for defendant OptionONE Healthcare Management, LLC. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Mr. Forhez is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/HK)

rulings.Case Title: ARROWOOD INDEMNITY COMPANY VS. MELISSA L GRIFFIN ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Thursday, August 30, 2018, Line 8, DEFENDANT MELISSA GRIFFIN's MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT. Defendant Melissa Griffin's motion for summary judgment on the sole cause of action for breach of contract alleged against her in the first amended complaint filed by plaintiff Arrowood Indemnity Company is denied. Ms. Griffin failed to satisfy her summary judgment burden of showing that Arrowood lacks standing to bring the breach of contract claim against her. Ms. Griffin's Facts 6 and 7 state that Arrowhead asserts that it has standing to sue on the three loan contracts due to an assignment from The Student Loan Corporation. However, Ms. Griffin does not have any facts in her separate statement showing that: 1)Citibank did not assign its rights to the three loan contracts to the SLC or an assignor of the SLC or 2) the SLC did not have any rights in the three loan contracts that it could assign to Arrowood. While it is undisputed that the SLC is not a signatory to the loan contracts, that fact is insufficient to prove that the SLC did not at some point obtain the rights of the lender in the three loan contracts and/or did not have any rights in those loan contracts to assign to Arrowood. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling adversely affects, in whole or in part, any party who is not represented by an attorney, then any other party (whether represented by an attorney or not) may submit on the tentative ruling without appearing for the hearing only if the adversely affected self-represented party has stated in writing or orally that it, he or she will not appear at the hearing. Counsel for Arrowood is required to prepare a proposed order which complies with CCP 437c(g) and repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/HK)

rulings.Case Title: ELECTRICAL INDUSTRY SERVICE BUREAU, INC. VS. THE TRAVELERS INDEMNITY COMPANY ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Thursday, August 30, 2018, Line 10, DEFENDANT THE TRAVELERS INDEMNITY COMPANY's DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT. Due to the unavailability of counsel for EISB on August 30, if the tentative ruling is contested, the hearing on this demurrer will be on September 4, 2018 or such other day as counsel for both sides agree. Defendant The Travelers Indemnity Company's demurrer to both causes of action alleged in the first amended complaint filed by plaintiff Electrical Industry Service Bureau, Inc. is sustained without leave to amend as to the first cause of action for failure to pay on a payment bond and overruled as to the second cause of action for violation of the Unfair Competition Law. EISB filed this lawsuit after the expiration of the limitations period for a claim for failure to pay on a payment bond. EISB's effort to be relieved of its untimely first cause of action based on equitable tolling is unavailing. EISB has not alleged that it filed a "claim" prior to filing of this lawsuit. Thus, for instance, in Structural Steel Fabricators, Inc. v. City of Orange (1995) 40 Cal. App. 4th 449, the "first claim" was a prior lawsuit. Liberally construed, the allegations in the FAC suggest that EISB may be seeking to invoke equitable estoppel as a basis to avoid the statute of limitations governing a claim for failure to pay on a payment bond, notwithstanding that EISB does not mention equitable estoppel in either the FAC or in its opposition memorandum. Nonetheless, EISB's allegations fall far short of what is required to adequately plead equitable estoppel. (Lantzy v. Centex Homes (2003) 31 Cal. 4th 363, 385). Citing Krantz v. BT Visual Images, L.L.C. (2001) 89 Cal. App. 4th 164, Travelers's sole argument in its initial memorandum as to the UCL claim is that the UCL claim fails because the payment bond claim failed. Not so. Unlike this case, Krantz did not involve the failure of an "antecedent claim" based on the statute of limitations. Per Business and Professions Code 17208, the UCL has its own statute of limitations and that four year limitations period, not the limitations period of the "antecedent" claim, generally governs the UCL claim. (Cortez v. Purolator Air Filtration Products Co. (2000) 23 Cal 4th 163, 178-79 (four year limitations period applies to UCL claim based on a violation of the Labor Code even though the Labor Code had a shorter limitations period)); see also Business and Professions Code 17205 (UCL claims are cumulative of claims under other California laws)). Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Travelers is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/HK)

rulings.Case Title: MELISSA BERRY ET AL VS. SUTTER VALLEY HOSPITALS, INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Defendants Motion To Compel Further Discovery Responses And Request For Monetary Sanctions
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Thursday, August 30, 2018, Line 3, DEFENDANT SUTTER VALLEY HOSPITALS, INC.'S Motion To Compel Further Discovery Responses And Request For Monetary Sanctions. Pro Tem Judge David McDonald, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Motion granted. While Plaintiffs did provide supplemental responses, they primarily consist of references to documents. Plaintiffs must provide additional supplemental responses by September 21, 2018 that specifically identify the facts that underlie the contentions in the complaint and the individuals with knowledge. Sanctions denied. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to davidmcdonald@prodigy.net with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: TIAN ZHANG VS. APEX CONSTRUCTION ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendant Bosco Lai'S Demurrer To Complaint
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Thursday, August 30, 2018, Line 14, Defendant Bosco Lai'S Demurrer To Complaint. The court re-affirms the tentative ruling issued prior to the August 27 hearing: Defendant Bosco Lai's demurrer to both causes of action in the complaint filed by plaintiff Tian Zhang is sustained with twenty days leave to amend as to both causes of action. The complaint fails to allege defendant Apex Construction's form of business entity. If it is alleged to be a sole proprietorship, then Mr. Lai is legally liable for all of its actions and inactions. If it is alleged to be an entity with separate legal significance, then Mr. Lai is legally liable for its actions and inactions only to the extent that Mr. Lai is the alter ego of Apex and/or personally committed tortious conduct. The complaint also fails to allege that Mr. Lai is the alter ego of Apex or that Mr. Lai personally committed tortious conduct. The complaint also fails to allege a connection between Apex and Mr. Lai to the premises sufficient to support imposition of premises liability. Mr. Zhang is given leave to remedy these deficiencies if he is able to do so. Counsel for plaintiff has submitted to the tentative ruling without need for a hearing. Counsel for Mr. Lai is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/HK)

rulings.Case Title: ALIGN TECHNOLOGY, INC. VS. HARVY WILLIAMS ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion For Assignment Of Rights, Restraining Order, And Turnover Order
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Thursday, August 30, 2018, Line 7, PLAINTIFF ALIGN TECHNOLOGY, INC.'S Motion For Assignment Of Rights, Restraining Order, And Turnover Order. Pro Tem Judge David McDonald, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Hearing required to ascertain compliance with the Court's order of August 20, 2018. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to davidmcdonald@prodigy.net with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: LEERINK REVELATION HEALTHCARE FUND I, L.P ET AL VS. BALAJI K SRINIVASAN ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Petition For Writ Of Attachment
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Thursday, August 30, 2018, Line 11, Petition For Writ Of Attachment. Hearing required. The court is particularly interested in hearing plaintiffs' response to the first argument in Mr. Srinivasan's supplemental opposition that, per the SPAs, the failure of the ROFR condition is triggered by the exercise of an RORF right, not the payment for the stock covered by the exercised right. =(302/HK)
